data.title: Arista Networks sees largely bullish views at BofA ahead of Q2 results
data.description:
BofA maintained Arista Networks' (ANET) Buy rating and $200 price objective ahead of the company's second quarter results on Aug. 4, citing its "expanding AI opportunity, Cloud Titan strength, and growing campus presence." However, the firm also highlighted margin pressure.

Shares of Arista climbed about 1% on Thursday.

"The stock is up 33% YTD versus NASDAQ up 11%, driven by accelerating AI networking demand. We focus on three factors into the print: (1) progress toward the $3.5bn AI fabric revenue target for 2026 (2) deferred revenue trends amid customers’ completion of DC buildouts, and (3) gross margin pressure from Cloud Titan mix and higher component costs," said analysts led by Tal Liani.

Big tech hyperscale data center operators are also called as Cloud Titans by some.

The analysts noted that they have modeled the second quarter revenue growth of 27% year-over-year, with a high bar of investor expectations for the full year 2026, modeled at 35% growth versus management's 28% guidance.

"Gross margin remains a key near-term swing factor as higher Cloud Titan mix and component costs offset strong revenue growth. We model 2Q gross margin of 62.5%, reflecting ~300bps of YoY pressure," said Liani and his team.

data.title: Galderma Group AG Non-GAAP EPS of $2.34, net sales of $3.13B
CrowdStrike extends slide for seventh straight session
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD) extended its recent decline on Thursday, with the stock declining 2.38% at $183.94 in afternoon trading, marking a seventh consecutive session in the red.

Shares have fallen 10.59% over the past six trading sessions, underperforming the S&P 500, which declined 0.60% over the same period. Separately, year to date, CRWD shares are up 58.85%, compared to a 9.55% gain for the S&P 500.

Despite the recent weakness in the stock, CRWD continues to expand its AI capabilities. The company  recently partnered with AI chipmaker Cerebras Systems to integrate Cerebras' inference technology into its Falcon AI Detection and Response platform, a move aimed at accelerating AI-powered threat detection while strengthening security across Cerebras' operations.

Seeking Alpha analyst Bay Area Ideas maintained a Hold rating on CrowdStrike, stating the company's leadership position in cybersecurity, expanding market opportunity, strong adoption, and solid financial performance. He added FY2027 guidance points to accelerating revenue and annual recurring revenue growth, with operating income and EPS expected to increase year over year. However, the analyst believes the stock's elevated valuation limits further upside potential, supporting the continued Hold rating.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings system assigned CRWD a Hold rating with a score of 3.48 out of 5. The stock receives an A grade for profitability and a B grade for growth, while valuation remains a key concern with an F grade.

Wall Street analysts remain largely positive, with 40 analysts assigning a Buy rating or higher, while 11 analysts maintain a Hold rating and one analyst rates the stock a Sell or lower. Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analysts maintain a more cautious view, with a Hold consensus rating on the shares.

data.title: Getty Realty anticipates $2.52-$2.54 2026 AFFO per share as it expands investment activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Our results for the second quarter reflect both of these dynamics as we increased our annualized base rent by 15%, grew our AFFO per share by 5.1% and increased our full year 2026 earnings guidance for the second time this year.” (President, CEO & Director Christopher Constant)
  • “Year-to-date, we have deployed more than $172 million at an initial cash yield of 7.6%.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • “At quarter end, our lease portfolio included 1,220 net lease properties and 1 active redevelopment site... occupancy was 99.8% and our weighted average lease term was 10.3 years.” (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Robert Ryan)
  • “AFFO per share was $0.62 in Q2 2026... As a result of our year-to-date investment activity, we are increasing our full year 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $2.52 to $2.54 from our prior guidance of $2.50 to $2.52.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Brian Dickman)

Outlook

  • “We are increasing our full year 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $2.52 to $2.54 from our prior guidance of $2.50 to $2.52.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “Our guidance reflects the current run rate from our in-place portfolio with certain expense and credit loss variability and does not include any prospective investment or capital activities.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing, management again emphasized that guidance excludes future deal activity, while pointing to higher year-to-date deployment and a larger closed investment quarter; in Q1, the company had raised guidance to “$2.50 to $2.52 from the prior range of $2.48 to $2.50.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Financial Results

  • “AFFO per share was $0.62 in Q2 2026 and $1.25 for the first half of 2026.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “That ratio was 9.3% for Q2 2026... we expect full-year G&A growth to be less than 2% and for our G&A ratio to fall below 9% as we continue to benefit from our efforts to scale the company while maintaining appropriate levels of overhead.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “We ended the quarter with approximately $1.1 billion of total debt outstanding... We have no debt maturities until June 2028.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “We currently have 5.8 million shares of common stock subject to outstanding forward sale agreements, which upon settlement are anticipated to raise gross proceeds of approximately $190.5 million.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Q&A

  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: Asked what is driving QSR momentum and whether the team has expanded; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “it’s the success of the person we brought on... it takes time to build relationships... we anticipate balanced volumes across the investment program.”
  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: Asked why Q2 cash yields were at the lower end versus “mid- to high 7%”; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “there’s a lot of volume in that kind of mid-7% range,” and Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman added, “the improving cost of capital... opening up opportunities for us to compete... our spreads have largely remained constant.”
  • Daniel Byun (for Jana Galan), Bank of America: Asked whether “$19.3 million advanced aggregate funding” is included in the $95 million under contract; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “No, that would have already been deployed.”
  • Daniel Byun (for Jana Galan), Bank of America: Asked about rent coverage and a higher sub-1x bucket; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “No, certainly no softening... same portfolio of ramping new-to-industry car washes.”
  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Asked whether improved cost of capital expanded the investment pool; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said, “it’s just opening up more opportunities,” and later President, CEO & Director Constant said, “what we’ve been messaging is what we’ve done over the last couple of years, we view as the floor.”
  • Robert Stevenson, Huntington: Asked about new asset types and scaling development funding; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “we’re trying to be really thoughtful as we think about extending beyond the 4 asset classes,” and on development funding, “we view it as another path to fee ownership and another path to growth.”
  • Robert Stevenson, Huntington: Asked whether Q2 dispositions were defensive; management responded that sales were selective, saying, “it was just $8 million, handful of properties... a mix.” (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Robert Ryan)
  • Robert Stevenson, Huntington: Asked where a new 10-year note would price; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “a 10-year note for us... would be about 6.25%.”
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked how to think about acquisition visibility versus reported pipeline; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said, “there’s always things that close that never hit the pipeline.”
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked about “the right level of forward liquidity”; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “the best word is balance,” adding the company does not want to be “too long, too much equity at a lower price.”
  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked how fuel price moves could affect coverage; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “margins continue to hold,” and “we’re not expecting to see any massive fluctuation.”
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG: Asked whether strong C-store margins are driving M&A and about geopolitical impacts; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “I don’t think today’s margin environment is really what’s driving increased M&A,” while adding operators’ “core businesses remain healthy.”
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG: Asked about credit losses and assumptions; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “No realized credit losses to date,” and “we continue to use a 25 basis point assumption.”
  • Wesley Golladay, Baird: Asked whether faster underwriting is due to volume or systems; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said, “candidly, I think it’s probably both.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive but probing on pricing, underwriting pace, tenant health buckets, funding strategy, and credit losses, including requests to “clarify” pipeline items and questions around cap rates, visibility, and coverage mix. (Multiple analysts)
  • Management tone was slightly positive and operationally confident in prepared remarks and generally steady in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to resilience and process advantages, including, “we are confident that we can deploy this capital in a productive and accretive manner.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • Versus the prior quarter, management used similar confidence language on portfolio durability and transaction markets, while Q2 added more detailed pushback on credit/coverage concerns, including, “No, certainly no softening.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management raised 2026 AFFO per share guidance again in Q2, moving from Q1’s “$2.50 to $2.52” to “$2.52 to $2.54,” and maintained that guidance excludes future investments and capital markets actions. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • The investment narrative shifted from Q1’s smaller quarter and larger under-contract figure to Q2’s materially higher closed activity and a smaller under-contract figure, with Q2 emphasizing increased velocity and funding capacity, including “more than $190 million of unsettled forward equity.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • Analysts’ focus stayed consistent quarter-to-quarter around pipeline momentum, cap-rate/yield bands, rent coverage buckets, and capital strategy; Q2 also added explicit questions on forward equity philosophy and debt pricing. (Multiple analysts)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged macro uncertainty, noting “economic volatility driven by geopolitical events,” while stating portfolio performance has remained resilient. (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • Credit and tenant health scrutiny centered on weaker rent-coverage buckets; management attributed this to “ramping new-to-industry car washes” and said, “nothing that’s causing us any great concern at this point.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • Capital markets sensitivity was discussed through the lens of rate levels and equity timing, with management cautioning against being “too long, too much equity at a lower price.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as a combination of steady in-place cash flows and accelerating external growth, highlighting higher year-to-date investment deployment, a largely full portfolio with long lease duration, and a second increase to full-year 2026 AFFO per share guidance. In Q&A, executives emphasized improving cost of capital, disciplined pricing in the mid-7% yield range, and stable tenant fundamentals, while addressing analyst scrutiny on rent-coverage buckets, forward equity strategy, and refinancing costs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Airlines anticipates full-year adjusted EPS to be break-even at midpoint amid $3.75/gal Q3 fuel forecast
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Airlines Group (AAL) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "American delivered a quarter that shows the commercial initiatives we've implemented over the past few years are producing meaningful results," said Robert Isom (CEO, President & Director), adding, "We achieved record quarterly revenue on year-over-year growth of more than 16%."

  • Isom said fuel remained the central headwind: "Executing solidly on that strategy in the second quarter helped offset nearly 50% of the $2.2 billion year-over-year increase in fuel expense," while adding, "We ended the second quarter with over $11 billion in available liquidity."

  • Nathaniel Pieper (Chief Commercial Officer) said revenue strength was broad-based: "Total revenue increasing 16.3% year-over-year," and added that "Domestic unit revenue increased nearly 11% year-over-year," with "Pacific unit revenue increased 15% year-over-year" and "Latin America unit revenue was up approximately 7% year-over-year."

  • Pieper highlighted operational/network actions tied to revenue quality, saying, "In April, we made significant changes to the bank structure at our largest hub, Dallas-Fort Worth," and "Misconnects across the system are down nearly 25% year-over-year."

  • Devon May (Executive VP & CFO) framed the quarter around fuel volatility and guidance resets: "In the second quarter, fuel expense increased by over $2.2 billion or 83% year-over-year," and "Since the beginning of July, expected third quarter fuel expense has increased by more than $700 million for the quarter."

Outlook

  • May said fuel assumptions drove capacity and earnings ranges: "As of July 21, we expect an average fuel price of approximately $3.75 per gallon in the third quarter," and "Our third quarter capacity is now expected to be up 3% to 5% year-over-year."

  • May guided to lower near-term profitability: "For the third quarter, we are guiding to an adjusted loss per diluted share of $0.70 to $0.10," and "we are adjusting our full year guidance to between a loss of $0.65 to a profit of $0.65 per diluted share."

  • Isom tied the full-year message directly to fuel: "Even against an expected nearly $6 billion year-over-year fuel headwind, we anticipate full year adjusted earnings to be break-even at the midpoint of our guidance range." Compared with Q1, management shifted from "we expect to be profitable in 2026" (Robert Isom, Q1) to break-even at the midpoint.

Financial Results

  • Isom emphasized operating cost control alongside revenue: "We remain focused on efficiency in the second quarter and held nonfuel year-over-year unit cost growth to under 3%," and he said the quarter ended with "over $11 billion in available liquidity."

  • May provided liquidity and balance-sheet direction: "We ended the quarter with $11.3 billion of liquidity," and "At the midpoint of our current 2026 guide, we expect to produce positive free cash flow for the year and to finish the year with lower net debt than at the start of the year."

  • On investment levels, May reiterated: "We continue to expect approximately $4 billion of capital expenditures" and "We expect to take 48 new aircraft this year."

Q&A

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: capacity urgency vs. fuel volatility? Robert Isom: "We're matching our network, our capacity to the demand environment," adding, "We're taking a diligent look at the fourth quarter right now."

  • Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: CapEx shape? Devon May: "We'll be right around $4 billion" in 2026 and "for next year, it's probably going to be somewhere around that $4.5 billion range."

  • Andrew Didora, BofA: 2027 labor/CASM? May: "The pilot agreement... becomes amendable in August of 2027" and "you should expect CASMx to grow at around low single digits" with mid-single-digit growth.

  • Didora, BofA: transatlantic trajectory? Pieper: "Our unit revenue in London was up 20% in the quarter" and said Atlantic performance into Q3 was seeing "good results."

  • John Godyn, Citi: premium strategy and loyalty? Pieper: "Our premium revenue in the quarter was up 19%," and "our enrollments are up 32% in the quarter year-over-year."

  • David Vernon, Bernstein: should AA trim network? Pieper: "You can't fight in every battle" and said the airline is "selectively cutting" and cited "trim... and reallocated" in Charlotte.

  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: labor-cost gap thesis and premium capacity risk? Isom: "Let's just be clear" and said the focus is "generating more revenue" while adding peers "haven't put" contracts "in place"; Pieper said, "Strong demand for premium products exist everywhere" and "I feel really comfortable with where we are."

  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley: what excites 2027+ and fuel pass-through? Isom: "We anticipate producing free cash flow" despite fuel being "up by over $6 billion," and said profitability is achievable "at any fuel pricing" with capacity and product value aligned.

  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: liquidity/leverage targets and merchandising? May: "We did a little over $1 billion, around $1.3 billion of incremental financings in Q2" and reiterated targets including "inside $35 billion of total debt" and leverage "inside of 3 turns"; Pieper said upsell gains came from evaluating "product offering" and noted an "instant upgrade program."

  • Alison Sider, WSJ: Southwest premium push impact? Pieper: "We're taking share. We're not losing it." Leslie Josephs, CNBC: fuel sourcing and actions? May: "We're around 65% supplied from the Gulf" and said the bigger lever is "how much capacity you want to produce."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, focused on structural profitability and capacity discipline, with questions like "why isn't the low-margin producer cutting capacity?" (Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI) and concerns about a "huge gap in financial performance" (David Vernon, Bernstein).

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks but more defensive in Q&A, using clarifiers such as "Let's just be clear" (Robert Isom, CEO, President & Director) and repeatedly anchoring answers on fuel volatility and revenue initiatives.

  • Versus Q1, management’s tone shifted from confidence in profitability ("we expect to be profitable in 2026," Robert Isom, Q1) to emphasizing near-term uncertainty ("The current fuel curve has dampened our near-term expectations," Devon May, CFO).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 introduced a sharper fuel-driven reset: May said, "Just 3 weeks ago, we were expecting to guide to full year pretax earnings approaching $1.5 billion," but updated to a full-year EPS range of (-$0.65) to $0.65.

  • Commercial momentum language strengthened, with Isom in Q2 stating, "We achieved record quarterly revenue," while Q1 stressed disruptions ("$320 million revenue impact from winter storms," Robert Isom, Q1).

  • Analysts’ focus broadened from Q1 pricing discipline and partnerships (including Alaska discussion) toward Q2 questions centered on capacity cuts, margin gap persistence, and liquidity under fuel volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Fuel price volatility dominated: May said expected Q3 fuel expense had increased "more than $700 million" since early July and cited "volatile fuel environment."

  • Capacity reductions risk unit costs: May said "CASMx in the quarter is slightly elevated due to the capacity reductions" and management will "continue to evaluate capacity levels based on both fuel prices and demand trends."

  • Competitive/structural questions persisted: analysts raised whether the network should be smaller and whether premium capacity could be "too much" (Jamie Baker, JPMorgan), while Pieper said the airline is "being very targeted" and "selectively cutting."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a record-revenue quarter driven by the four-pillar commercial plan, but repeatedly framed the investment case around fuel volatility resetting near-term earnings expectations; leadership maintained that revenue momentum, cost discipline, and planned premium/network initiatives can support positive free cash flow and balance-sheet improvement even as guidance ranges widened and capacity plans were adjusted in response to higher expected fuel prices.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PCA expects Q3 2026 EPS of $2.91 as pricing increases roll in
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Packaging Corporation of America (PKG) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "Yesterday, we reported second quarter net income of $192 million or $2.15 per share." (Chairman of the Board & CEO Mark Kowlzan)
  • "Excluding special items, the second quarter 2026 net income was $210 million or $2.35 per share" and "Second quarter net sales were $2.5 billion" and "Total company EBITDA for the second quarter, excluding special items, was $486 million." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "We exceeded our guidance of $2.33 on the strength of our corrugated volumes" and "Greif's earnings contribution also exceeded our expectations." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Operational performance was a mixed bag during the quarter" and "we were hit with some production interruptions resulting from utility power outages across the mill system" and "This further emphasizes our need to execute and realize the benefits of the gas turbine projects." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Demand was very strong throughout the quarter across our entire customer base" and "Shipments were up over 24% in total and per day versus last year, with the legacy business up 4.1%, achieving an all-time record for total quarterly shipments." (President Thomas Hassfurther)
  • "I'm pleased to report that we successfully started up the new Ohio plant earlier this month ahead of schedule." (President Hassfurther)
  • "Cash provided by operations was $376 million and after $206 million of CapEx, free cash flow was $170 million." (Executive VP & CFO Kent Pflederer)

Outlook

  • "Considering these items, we expect third quarter earnings of $2.91 per share, excluding special items." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "We expect continued strong demand in the Packaging segment and corrugated products volume to increase with 1 more shipping day." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Prices for containerboard and corrugated products will be higher as we complete implementation of our first announced increase and begin to realize our second announced price increase." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Costs for freight across the business will remain at or around the elevated levels we experienced in May and June" and "Recycled fiber prices are continuing to increase." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Our outage expense was $0.34 during the second quarter" and "Our back half estimates are now $0.30 for the third quarter and $0.63 for the fourth quarter, totaling $1.41 for the year." (CFO Pflederer)

Financial results

  • "Second quarter net income included special items expense of $0.20 per share, primarily for costs and write-offs related to facilities closures, the Wallula mill restructuring charges and costs related to the acquisition and integration of the Greif containerboard business." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "The decrease in the legacy earnings was driven primarily by higher freight costs of $0.26" and "lower price and mix in the packaging business, $0.11" and "higher corporate and other expenses, $0.12." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Packaging business. EBITDA, excluding special items in the second quarter of 2026 of $489 million with sales of $2.3 billion resulted in a margin of 21.1%." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "We produced 1,415,000 tons of containerboard during the quarter" and "System-wide, our inventories were down 25,000 tons from the end of the first quarter." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "Looking at the Paper segment, EBITDA, excluding special items in the second quarter was $39 million with sales of $157 million, or a 24.9% margin." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "We did not repurchase shares during the quarter." (CFO Pflederer)

Q&A

  • George Staphos, BofA Securities: "can you give us a rundown on what you're seeing in terms of bookings and billings to start the third quarter?" President Hassfurther: "billings are up 1.5% so far" and "we're expecting for the quarter, this is in the legacy business and up about 2%."
  • Michael Roxland, Truist: "any color you could provide on the miss in your legacy business relative to expectations?" CFO Pflederer: "Greif was a $0.14 earnings contribution" and "$0.04 of that was the depreciation benefit" and "that was driven by largely higher volumes than we expected and very good operational performance."
  • Michael Roxland, Truist: "any thoughts around those tariffs" on Canadian imports? CFO Pflederer: "our initial read is little to no impact on PCA" and "we don't view it as a significant driver either way."
  • Mark Weintraub, Seaport: "how much of it then of the ... $50 per ton would have shown up" in Q2 and timing of the next increase? CFO Pflederer: "the vast majority of that's coming in Q3" and "maybe 70%, 75% of it's Q3" and "The second increase, the majority will come in, in 4Q."
  • Gabe Hajde, Wells Fargo: "how would you describe ... the feel in the market" on supply-demand? President Hassfurther: "tight."
  • Gabe Hajde, Wells Fargo: "maintenance outages" and grid volatility? CEO Kowlzan: "we had at least 5 distinct utility power outage situations" and "it also speaks to the importance of the 3 gas turbine projects ... over the next 2 years."
  • Anojja Shah, UBS: "any update you can give us on the Greif synergies?" CFO Pflederer: "we're on track probably to exceed the $30 million run rate by the end of the year."
  • Anthony Pettinari, Citi: cadence of gas turbines and CapEx direction? CEO Kowlzan: "The goal would be to have that gas turbine at Jackson up online next year" and for DeRidder and Riverville "it's probably going to be more like the first part of 2028 to the mid part of 2028" and "I would assume that the CapEx is going to stay in this range."
  • Hillary Cacanando, Deutsche Bank: need more price increases? President Hassfurther: "we don't comment on price going forward."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative, centered on cost inflation, price realization timing, and market tightness, with questions probing downside and sustainability including "any color ... on the miss in your legacy business" (Roxland) and pass-through and pricing power (Weintraub) and tariffs (Roxland).
  • Management tone slightly positive in prepared remarks with demand and integration emphasis, alongside operational caution on utilities and costs, including "Demand was very strong" (President Hassfurther) and "Operational performance was a mixed bag" (CEO Kowlzan) and "tight" (President Hassfurther).
  • Compared with Q1, management messaging shifted from "Operational performance during the quarter was exceptional" (CEO Kowlzan, Q1) to highlighting grid-driven disruptions and mitigation via turbine projects (CEO Kowlzan, Q2), while analysts continued to press on Greif contribution, pricing implementation, and inflation.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 results reflected a Greif swing from "generated a loss of $0.06 during the first quarter" (CEO Kowlzan, Q1) to "$0.14 of earnings from the acquired Greif business" (CEO Kowlzan, Q2), with Q2 also including a stated "$0.04 benefit" tied to depreciation measurement adjustments (CEO Kowlzan, Q2).
  • Demand commentary remained strong, with Q1 noting "corrugated shipments per day were up 2.8%" in legacy (President Hassfurther, Q1) and Q2 noting "Shipments were up over 24% in total and per day versus last year" including the acquisition (President Hassfurther, Q2).
  • Capital and reliability emphasis broadened from Q1 board approvals for turbines at Jackson and Riverville and scoping DeRidder (CEO Kowlzan, Q1) to Q2 detailing Jackson construction and permitting-driven delays for DeRidder/Riverville to 2028 timing (CEO Kowlzan, Q2).

Risks and concerns

  • "Costs for freight across the business will remain at or around the elevated levels" and "Recycled fiber prices are continuing to increase." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "production interruptions resulting from utility power outages" with mitigation via "the gas turbine projects, which will reduce, if not eliminate, our reliance on the grid at 3 key facilities." (CEO Kowlzan)
  • "we decided mid-quarter to lower export sales to build inventory" to support internal supply priorities. (President Hassfurther)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 around strong corrugated demand and a higher-than-expected Greif contribution, while flagging elevated freight and rising recycled fiber costs and operational volatility from utility outages. Heading into Q3, management guided to "third quarter earnings of $2.91 per share, excluding special items" (CEO Kowlzan) on higher realized pricing and continued tight market conditions, alongside lower packaging outage impact but continued cost pressure in freight and recycled fiber.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Novocure outlines 2026 revenue of $710M-$725M while targeting adjusted EBITDA of $0M-$15M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NovoCure (NVCR) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “This was our strongest commercial quarter to date. With record net revenues and active patients on therapy, we continue to build on the momentum of the first quarter and have also made meaningful progress on our path to profitability.” (Executive Chairman William Doyle)
  • “Starting with Optune Gio, we finished the quarter with 4,636 active patients on therapy, an increase of 11% year-over-year.” (Executive Chairman Doyle) “A key driver this quarter was U.S. growth, where active patients increased 8% year-over-year.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)
  • “We received 418 prescriptions in the quarter and 285 Optune Pax patients were on therapy as of June 30.” (Executive Chairman Doyle) “We are encouraged by the adoption of Optune Pax at both academic and community practices.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)
  • “Earlier this month, we received CE Mark for Optune Pax… concomitant with gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel in accordance with local guidelines.” (Executive Chairman Doyle) “We have since finalized labeling and registration in Germany and begun certifying prescribers.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)
  • “Turning to Optune Lua. We finished the period with 207 patients on therapy.” (Executive Chairman Doyle) “As of June 30, we had 64 Optune Lua patients on therapy in Japan.” (Executive Chairman Doyle)
  • “The FDA review of Optune Maia… remains on track for a Q4 decision.” (Executive Chairman Doyle) “Earlier this month, the NCCN updated guidelines… and included Optune Maia as a Category 2A recommended treatment option…” (Executive Chairman Doyle)
  • “With 3 products approved and under FDA review, we are shifting that mandate to focus on strengthening our market position and broadening labels.” (CEO Frank Leonard) “Our near-term focus is pancreatic cancer.” (CEO Leonard)
  • “We believe these changes will cut the total spend on LUNAR by approximately $90 million with the savings allocated to pancreatic cancer programs.” (CEO Leonard)
  • “Net revenue in the second quarter was $184 million, an increase of 16% year-over-year.” (Chief Financial Officer Christoph Brackmann) “We are updating our full year revenue guidance to a range of $710 million to $725 million…” (CFO Brackmann)

Outlook

  • “Based on the strength of our commercial performance in Q2, we are updating our full year revenue guidance to a range of $710 million to $725 million, representing 8% to 11% growth.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • “We are also updating our guidance range for combined revenue from Optune Lua and Optune Pax to $20 million to $30 million for the year.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • “We are updating our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance this morning to a range from $0 million to $15 million.” (CFO Brackmann) “The first milestone in our path to profitability is to reach breakeven on an adjusted EBITDA level, which we now plan to achieve for the full year of 2026.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • Compared with Q1 2026 guidance language, management moved from “a range from negative $15 million to breakeven” (CFO Brackmann) to “a range from $0 million to $15 million” (CFO Brackmann), and from “$690 million to $710 million” (CFO Brackmann) to “$710 million to $725 million” (CFO Brackmann).

Financial results

  • “Gross margin in the quarter was 78% compared to 74% in Q2 of 2025.” (CFO Brackmann) “This was primarily due to a tariff refund of $5 million as well as lower array costs due to improved utilization and manufacturing efficiencies.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • “We expect quarterly gross margins to remain in the mid-70s through year-end 2026 as we bring more Optune Pax patients on therapy prior to establishing broad reimbursement.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • “Research and development costs in the quarter were $51 million…” (CFO Brackmann) “Sales and marketing expenses in Q2 were $62 million…” (CFO Brackmann) “G&A costs in the quarter were $40 million…” (CFO Brackmann)
  • “Our net loss for the quarter was $16 million…” (CFO Brackmann) “Loss per share in the quarter was $0.13.” (CFO Brackmann) “Adjusted EBITDA in the quarter was $11 million…” (CFO Brackmann)
  • “Our cash and investment balance as of June 30, 2026, was $441 million.” (CFO Brackmann)

Q&A

  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked whether Optune Pax’s Q2 trajectory is sustainable and where growth is coming from; CEO Leonard said, “we are pleased with the launch,” and added, “we are right now looking at about half of our prescriber base has already become a repeat prescriber,” with a Q3 focus to “push that number of repeat prescribers up.” (CEO Leonard)
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked why an IDE trial with RAS inhibitors and whether a registrational study is needed; CEO Leonard said, “The fastest way… is… through an IDE trial,” adding, “we are opening this trial in order to look at safety and feasibility,” and “We are not in metastatic disease looking to move towards a registrational trial…” (CEO Leonard)
  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: asked about Rx-to-active conversion and whether Pax fill rates differ from Gio; CEO Leonard said, “I wouldn't begin trying to glean specific statistics from the numbers,” and added, “it's a shorter time between prescription and start than what we typically see in GBM.” (CEO Leonard)
  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: questioned why the updated revenue guide implies moderation; CFO Brackmann cited “$3 million in onetime items” and said “the stars aligned in Q2,” adding, “we also wanted to respect that we had a fantastic quarter in Q2.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: asked about Lua demand and second-half outlook; CFO Brackmann said, “We are very pleased with the launch,” and flagged contracting as “a gating item” despite “national reimbursement.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan: pressed for total Optune Pax prescribers; CEO Leonard said, “We haven't given the specific number of certified prescribers,” and described a range where “some doctors have written more than 10 prescriptions.” (CEO Leonard)
  • Kevin DeGeeter, Ladenburg Thalmann: asked about Germany go-to-market and reimbursement; CEO Leonard said Germany allows “case-by-case reimbursement,” and CFO Brackmann sized the on-label opportunity: “The TAM in the U.S. on label is 15,000 patients annually… And in Germany… 4,000 patients on label.” (CFO Brackmann)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated launch-focused probing; examples included “Script volumes up sequentially in the triple digits… Is this sustainable… for back half?” (Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI) and “what's the total number of prescribers… sourcing those 418 Pax scripts” (Jessica Fye, JPMorgan).
  • Management’s tone was positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks, including “we are updating our full year revenue guidance” (CFO Brackmann) and “we are shifting… to focus on strengthening our market position and broadening labels” (CEO Leonard), while staying measured in Q&A with lines such as “we are not giving context right now on the total number of certifications” (CEO Leonard).
  • Compared with Q1, Q2 included more explicit profitability framing and cost actions, including “we believe these changes will cut the total spend on LUNAR by approximately $90 million” (CEO Leonard) and “we now plan to achieve [breakeven] for the full year of 2026” (CFO Brackmann).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategy emphasis shifted from awaiting GBM catalyst TRIDENT (“top line data… expected in the second quarter” (CEO Leonard, Q1)) to reallocating lung trial resources and dollars, with Q2 detailing “reallocating clinical resources… discontinuing the trial at lower enrollment sites” and aiming to “reduce the patient sample size.” (CEO Leonard)
  • Optune Pax launch discussion evolved from early funnel metrics in Q1 (“we certified 868 health care providers… received 169 prescriptions… completed 90 patient starts” (Executive Chairman Doyle, Q1)) to repeat-prescriber dynamics in Q2 (“about half of our prescriber base has already become a repeat prescriber” (CEO Leonard)).
  • Guidance language moved higher in Q2, with management stating “updating our full year revenue guidance” (CFO Brackmann) and “updating our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance” (CFO Brackmann) versus Q1’s lower ranges. (CFO Brackmann)

Risks and concerns

  • Reimbursement and contracting remained a near-term constraint outside the U.S., with management noting Japan requires “additional contracting… at each treatment site” (Executive Chairman Doyle) and describing contracting as “a gating item.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • Gross margin variability was tied to launch and reimbursement timing, as CFO Brackmann said margins should stay “in the mid-70s… as we bring more Optune Pax patients on therapy prior to establishing broad reimbursement.” (CFO Brackmann)
  • Clinical execution and cost control were framed as mitigations, including “operating expense discipline” (CEO Leonard) and “cost optimizing projects like the LUNAR-2 trial.” (CFO Brackmann)

Final takeaway

NovoCure’s management described Q2 as its “strongest commercial quarter to date” (Executive Chairman Doyle) and raised full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance while tying profitability goals to expense discipline and a reallocation of clinical spend—most notably, a LUNAR-2 redesign expected to reduce total trial spend by “approximately $90 million” (CEO Leonard) and fund expanded pancreatic cancer studies and combinations, alongside continued execution on Optune Pax uptake, Japan’s Optune Lua rollout, and a “Q4 decision” timeline for Optune Maia. (Executive Chairman Doyle)

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rollins forecasts at least 6% organic growth and at least 10% incremental margins for 2026 amid softer residential demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rollins, Inc. (ROL) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Our second quarter results did not meet our expectations, driven primarily by slower growth within certain portions of our residential pest control business.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Jerry Gahlhoff)
  • “The pressure was concentrated in brands such as Orkin that rely more heavily on consumer-initiated demand through search, digital media and inbound calls.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff)
  • “Brands that generate customers through relationship-based channels like direct sales, door-to-door models, and relationships with homebuilders delivered organic growth above our targeted 7% to 8% range for the quarter.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff)
  • “At Orkin, for example, we recently promoted Scott Weaver to Chief Operating Officer of Orkin North America.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff)
  • “Total revenue increased 7.9%, while organic growth was 5.7%, both below our expectations for the quarter.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer William Harkins)

Outlook

  • “We are updating our full year outlook. We now expect organic growth of at least 6% for the year and incremental margins of at least 10% for 2026, with implied margin improvement in the back half of the year to be Q4-weighted.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “Importantly, the revision to our 2026 expectations reflects our current assessment of near-term operating conditions rather than any change to the medium-term algorithm we outlined at our Investor Day in May.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “We continue to believe this business is capable of generating organic growth of at least 7% while delivering meaningful margin expansion.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • Change vs. the prior quarter call: “We continue to expect organic growth in the 7% to 8% range for the year with growth from M&A of 2% to 3%.” (Executive VP, CFO Kenneth Krause, Q1 2026)

Financial Results

  • “In the second quarter, residential revenues increased 6.6%. Commercial pest control increased 8.6% and termite and ancillary increased 10.5%.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “Gross margin was 52.8%, a decrease of 100 basis points.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “Second quarter GAAP operating income was $201 million… Adjusted operating income was $210 million… and second quarter adjusted EBITDA was $236 million… which represented a 21.9% margin.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “Quarterly GAAP net income was $144 million or $0.30 per share… adjusted net income for the quarter was $152 million or $0.32 per share.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “We generated operating cash flow of $173 million and free cash flow of $166 million… Free cash flow conversion… was above 115% for the quarter.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)

Q&A

  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair & Company L.L.C., Research Division: “Have you seen any changes in retention or pricing?” Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff: “We’ve not seen any hesitancy from our customers on pricing… our customer retention remains strong.”
  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair & Company L.L.C., Research Division: “Have there been any changes in SEO or from the LLM overviews that are impacting leads?” Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff: “We just had fewer people year-over-year, actively searching the digital channel for pest control needs.”
  • Curtis Nagle, BofA Securities, Research Division: “recurring revenue versus one-time within residential, what did that look like in the quarter?” Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff: “The recurring has been healthier… The one-time… has been the brunt of it, of the miss.”
  • Gregory Parrish, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: incremental margin guide vs. execution levers; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins: “we didn’t expect to be posting an incremental margin of 6.5% in the second quarter.”
  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: “should think about floor” on 10% incrementals; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins: “we believe that’s going to be the floor… it will be weighted to the fourth quarter.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with repeated pressure on the residential slowdown and visibility, including “what market share changes did you observe?” (Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division) and “what to make of that?” (Jason Haas, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division).
  • Management: neutral to slightly positive in tone while acknowledging a miss; “we’re cautious with respect to near-term trends,” but “we were encouraged that inbound lead flow and call center volumes improved towards the end of June.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff)
  • Versus last quarter: Q1 prepared remarks were more upbeat—“I’m pleased to report Rollins delivered strong first quarter results”—and guidance language was firmer on “organic growth in the 7% to 8% range.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff; Executive VP, CFO Krause, Q1 2026)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategy emphasis shifted from Q1 peak-season staffing and broad-based growth (“We are well staffed… ready to provide an exceptional level of service”) to Q2 execution changes in residential acquisition and accountability (“organizational and operational changes designed to strengthen accountability”). (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff)
  • Guidance language shifted from Q1 “organic growth in the 7% to 8% range” to Q2 “organic growth of at least 6%” and “incremental margins of at least 10%… Q4-weighted.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins; Executive VP, CFO Krause, Q1 2026)
  • Analyst focus moved from early-year weather normalization and insurance/claims volatility to digital lead volumes, one-time demand weakness, and how LLM/AI could be affecting top-of-funnel behavior. (Current Q&A)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Customer demand patterns have been more variable to start peak season than we’ve experienced in the better part of a decade.” (Principal Executive Officer, President, CEO & Director Gahlhoff)
  • “Higher medical-related costs and fuel headwinds pressured quarterly margins.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)
  • “Q3 of last year had a lot of favorability in the numbers… we’ve got a pretty difficult number to hurdle as we go into Q3 of this year.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Harkins)

Final Takeaway

Management said Q2 underperformed due to a concentrated slowdown in consumer-initiated residential demand at brands like Orkin, while relationship-driven channels (including door-to-door and homebuilder partnerships) and termite/ancillary and commercial remained growth contributors. The company updated its 2026 outlook to at least 6% organic growth and at least 10% incremental margins, with improvement “Q4-weighted,” and highlighted operational changes at Orkin (including Scott Weaver’s promotion) alongside cost actions aimed at acquisition efficiency, labor alignment, and medical and fleet expense management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
